data.title: Oscar Health: A Misunderstood Platform
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Alpha Line as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

The Investment Idea

While consensus views Oscar Health (OSCR) as a volatile Affordable Care Act (ACA)-focused insurer subject to regulatory risk and earnings volatility, this storyline overlooks the more basic structural change underway. Below its headline sector screen P/E multiples, Oscar is increasingly becoming an all-in-one healthcare infrastructure and data platform. In its model, other than its optics of valuation, Oscar is less cyclical and less exposed to sheltered price and usage shocks than competition. With a spirited post earnings run-up, Wall Street is catching on, but not fast enough.

This is no longer just a 2026 ACA bet. Oscar's moat foundation is being constructed on ICHRA adoption, a 2x operating leverage assumption by virtue of technology-driven SG&A compression, and a SaaS-like platform, +Oscar, insuring 500K+ of external lives. As peers cut costs reactively, Oscar's margin appreciation is already baked. Investors who obsess about valuation multiples, without highlighting a lack of capital efficiency and policy economics, risk overlooking the built-in upside potential beyond 2026.

Strategic Evolution: From Health Plan to Operating System

Its earliest years were not very different from defensive ACA expansion and underwriting losses, having attracted skepticism about being a disrupter supported by venture capital. But from 2022 onwards, its business model was transformed. CEO Mark Bertolini’s vision was a sustainably profitable actuary-driven model of absorptions of fixed costs and platform economics. This was not a cost-cutting program, this was a meticulous conversion from defensive health insurance to future health management infrastructure on the basis of AI.

Evidence of that maturity was evident in the company’s SG&A ratio falling 260 bps year-over-year in Q1 2025 to 15.8%, a low for the company. That first-time achievement two years prior to internal plans shows system efficiency from Oscar’s proprietary modular technology stack, not periodic layoffs or advertising decreases. As of March 2025, the company had over 2 million effectuated members, up 41% year-over-year, with good retention and new enrollments in its IFP and ICHRA segments.

Strategically, Oscar is betting on ICHRA as the wedge to unlock employer-sponsored disruption. This is not just market expansion, it’s a structural redefinition of insurance portability and pricing mechanics. With approximately 75 million SMB employees addressable by 2027 and early pilots proving cost savings for both employers and chronically ill patients, Oscar’s long-term growth narrative rests on flipping the traditional group insurance model on its head.

While that’s happening, +Oscar, Oscar’s outgoing technology platform, lies dormant to develop slowly into an operating system for third-party payers, IDNs, and ACOs. Already home to more than 500,000 non-Oscar lives, +Oscar is scaling a set of campaign tools and customized care pathways with proven ROI: 6x pharmacy refill switch return on investment, 48% higher booking of appointments, and higher retention in Medicare Advantage pilots.

Oscar’s AI Leverage Is Already in the Margins

In Q1 2025, Oscar generated $328.8 million of Adjusted EBITDA (+50% year-over-year), with net income of $275.3 million on $3 billion of revenues, a 9.8% operating margin, 110 basis points higher year-over-year. These are numbers more characteristic of an emerging healthtech platform, not a typical MLR-burdened insurer.

SG&A leverage is structural, not cyclical. 40% of Q1 cost ratio improvement came from absorption of fixed costs, and 15% came from technology-enabled automation, including AI-powered member chatbots and automation of EHR documentation. These are not quick fixes but compound levers that are scalable. Provider response time decreased by 28% through AI-powered triage, and over 98% of claims were automatically adjudicated.

Oscar’s MLR edged higher modestly 75.4% (up 120 basis points year-over-year), primarily due to a $31 million prior period risk adjusted payable, not underlying worsening. This was partially offset by favorable claims runoff and a CSR rebound. Be aware that elevated Q1 utilizations need to be matched by favorable risk adjusted in other quarters, and management already indicated they are putting on medical cost control levers not in guidance.

Free cash quality deserves a special emphasis. Operating cash flow was at $878.5 million during Q1, net cash buildup of $709 million pushing cash and investments to around $5 billion. Such increases are organically being reinvested, with around $9 million CapEx and without dilutionary financing, preference being given to organic growth and data infrastructure addition. Oscar’s insurance units had $907 million of excess capital, providing full reinvestment and reserve freedom for the company.

Competitive Positioning: A Healthcare Stack Company, Not a Carrier

Oscar Health’s competitive positioning becomes clearer when examined against both legacy insurers and emerging platform players. Compared to incumbents like Centene and Elevance, which operate diversified insurance portfolios across Medicaid, Medicare, and employer-sponsored plans, Oscar is strategically focused on the ACA individual market and early ICHRA adoption, segments with high-growth potential and limited innovation from peers.

While insurance firms such as Principal Financial and Corebridge are asset-weighted annuities and group benefits leaders, Oscar resembles a health tech firm rather than a conventional underwriter. Its +Oscar platform has over 500,000 external lives under administration and offers infrastructure-as-a-service at the ACO and IDN level, a blueprint none of its insurance peers can replicate. Few of the public digital health firms even put underwriting, platform economics, and B2B SaaS under a single architecture.

Oscar’s moat exists within this two-engine model: it captures ACA and ICHRA share with an efficient insurance platform while externalizing its stack with +Oscar. Traditional insurers are fundamentally slower because of legacy IT, compliance overhead, and capital-intensive businesses. But with a data-driven stack, no leverage, and $2.99 billion cash, Oscar has flexibility and capital productivity and is positioned to capture share from traditional insurers as well as under-optimized digital players.

The ACA market has grown from a subscale experiment to a giant $100 billion-plus addressable industry, and Oscar has positioned itself as a top-5 player with roughly 7% share and an 18-state presence. However, Oscar’s approach significantly differs from peers such as Centene or Elevance, which rely on wide Medicaid and broad commercial diversification.

Oscar’s thrust is surgically focused, vertically integrated, and further becoming bifurcated across two distinct tracks of growth: ACA-ICHRA hybrid insurance and B2B tech through +Oscar. Further, the projected surge in ICHRA enrollment from 2.5 million in 2024 to 15 million by 2032 significantly expands Oscar Health’s addressable market, reinforcing its strategy as an early mover in this employer-funded, consumer-directed insurance model.

Within its core insuring business, disciplined market expansion is underway at Oscar, with a 150+ new MSAs by 2027 goal and 13%-18% market share growth in current markets. Culturally fitted products (e.g., HolaOscar for Spanish-speaking individuals) are supplemented by AI-fueled navigation tools to drive NPS, engagement, and retention. Open enrollment 2024 retention reached 82%, and NPS for virtual PCPs goes beyond 75, never seen in insurance.

At the same time, Oscar’s +Oscar platform is positioning itself as a SaaS-like layer of infrastructure for ACOs and community health plans. Here, rather than the difference being technology alone, its outcomes. +Oscar’s automation engine already drives more than 20 of its highly valuable AI application examples, along patient intake through to utilisation management, so can deliver obvious cost savings to third-party customers, de-risking their value-based healthcare transition. Omnichannel health engagement is supported by the platform, along with sophisticated admin workflow automation, basically transferring Oscar’s internal cost benefit to an external revenue stream.

This two-engine construct, ACA footprint and technology externalization, cannot be easily matched and increasingly not easily challenged. This legacy health plan's capital intensity begets inertia. Oscar having spent upfront pays margin compounding without CapEx bloat. With static infrastructure more or less in place, the incremental profit by each new member or external life is disproportionately incremental profit.

Oscar’s Valuation Makes No Sense And That’s the Opportunity

Oscar’s valuation defies easy categorization. At a forward P/E of 30, it appears overvalued compared to sector medians near 11x. However, this view ignores Oscar’s transition from transactional insurance to a recurring-margin platform. A more appropriate lens would triangulate valuation on normalized free cash flow, Adjusted EBITDA trajectory, and revenue composition quality.

At a ~$21 share price and 268.9 million diluted shares outstanding, Oscar Health’s market capitalization stands at approximately $4.88 billion. With $2.99 billion in total cash and $359 million in debt, Oscar’s enterprise value is around $3.01 billion, reflecting a modest valuation for a business generating strong cash flow and expanding margins.

This translates to an EV/Sales multiple of just ~0.27x based on 2025 revenue guidance of $11.25 billion, and an EV/EBITDA multiple of ~7.7x using the $390 million midpoint of its 2025 Adjusted EBITDA outlook. At the low end of the guide, the EV/EBITDA multiple is ~8.25x; at the high end, ~7.25x, still well below the 15–20x multiples often assigned to profitable digital health platforms and infrastructure-grade insurers.

A 3–5 year bull case rests on sustained 20% revenue CAGR (as guided) with SG&A compression and ICHRA/tech monetization optionality. If Oscar sustains 5%+ operating margins and rerates toward 18–20x EBITDA, the equity could double or triple from current levels. Conversely, a bear case rooted in policy volatility or ACA deflation still finds some downside cushion in the 0.05x EV/Sales multiple, a floor-like valuation for a profitable business with no debt maturity concerns and a fortress balance sheet.

Additionally, in arriving at an intrinsic value, I built a 5-year discounted cash flow (DCF) based on free cash flow. While Q1 2025 had $878.5 million of cash flow from operations, I normalize FY2025 free cash flow at $950 million, less than the $3 billion+ implied annualizing Q1 in an attempt to eliminate seasonal risk adjust distortions as well as account for conservative working capital assumptions. This point nevertheless still indicates healthy year-over-year growth from a healthy $487 million in FY2023.

This starting point still denotes healthy year-over-year growth from $487 million FY2023. Using a 30% FCF CAGR is justified not by past FCF trends alone, but by Oscar’s structural shift from cash-burning insurer to a scalable platform. While revenue has grown at ~30% CAGR, free cash flow inflected sharply in 2023 and surged further in Q1 2025, reflecting strong operating leverage and minimal CapEx.

With SG&A compression, tech automation, and high-margin platform monetization, FCF is now compounding faster than revenue. This makes a 30% FCF growth rate both realistic and conservative for a platform still early in its scaling curve. As a result, I use a 30% annual FCF CAGR through FY2029, warranted by Oscar’s ~30% historic revenue growth CAGR, widening margins, and platform-like scalability.

The present value of free cash flows from 2025–2029 totals approximately $6.02 billion using an 11% WACC. Applying a 5x terminal FCF multiple to the 2029 figure gives a terminal value of $17.5 billion, which when discounted to present is worth $10.39 billion. Combined, the enterprise value totals $16.41 billion. After adjusting for cash and debt, and dividing by 268.9 million diluted shares, we estimate a fair value of approximately $61 per share, implying more than 3x upside from current levels.

Year Free Cash Flow Discount Factor (11%) Discounted FCF
2025 950 0.90 855.86
2026 1235 0.81 1002.35
2027 1605.5 0.73 1173.93
2028 2087.15 0.66 1374.87
2029 2713.295 0.59 1610.21
Total $6017.22

This base case assumes no +Oscar external monetization and gradual ICHRA ramp. Already with SG&A squeezing and capex negligible, Oscar is a capital-light stack of infrastructure mispriced as a legacy insurer, creating a re-rating opportunity as its repeat-margin model emerges.

What Could Go Wrong?

Oscar Health’s execution relies heavily on predictable ACA policy support and sustained federal subsidies; any rollback will put pressure on enrollment and margins. While its ICHRA wedge has promise, employer adoption may take longer than assumed if brokers or HR gatekeepers are opposed to moving away from legacy group plans.

Operationally, a high medical loss ratio or a sudden utilization spike can undermine the structural margin improvements driving the free cash flow narrative. The +Oscar platform provides optionality but also brings third-party technology and service delivery risks, particularly as it goes beyond internal use. Competition is also increasing, with larger incumbents investing in parallel digital tooling and vertical integration, potentially reducing Oscar’s price advantage.

Despite the company’s fortress balance sheet with ~$3 billion of cash and negligible debt, its valuation rerating relies upon sustained margin scale and rerate multiple the market may not support over the short term if execution disappoints. Overall, the balance of risks is moderate but manageable with disciplined cost leverage, differentiated technology stack, and structural tailwinds from individual market growth.

Conclusion

Finally, Oscar Health is not acting like a platform company, but it’s acting like one. As it continues to shrink SG&A, grow ICHRA, and replicate +Oscar into a system of modular health infrastructure businesses, the current-frame lens of valuation multiples and regulatory news buzz is due for a re-calibration. Investors who look past the noise are discovering a company stealthily creating mission-critical stature in the ACA’s most under-appreciated frontier.

data.title: The Rise Of Physical Gold And Silver Over Paper Assets
data.description:
From Paper To Physical

My main goal with this article is to highlight the key differences between physical gold (XAUUSD:CUR) and silver (XAGUSD:CUR) versus paper assets like futures, options, ETFs, bonds or equities. It all comes down to counterparty risks, but I hope to provide a more rounded discussion on this increasingly important topic. After all, gold and silver deliveries on the COMEX are at all-time highs. And why would Germany and Italy want to repatriate $245 billion worth of gold stored in the Fed's vaults of New York? Why now? What's the rush?

In my view, any discussion about precious metals is incomplete without a thorough understanding of the relationship between physical and paper metal. Within the two, which one has the greatest power to set prices? If paper gold has mostly set the tone over the last few decades, things are starting to change. As I tried to highlight in an article last month, the setup could be even more explosive with silver. My knowledge about Platinum (XPTUSD:CUR) and Palladium (XPDUSD:CUR) is still limited, so this is a market segment that I plan to research in more detail soon.

The rising industrial demand is acting like a tightening screw on spot prices. Over the last few years, an increase in recycling has kept a lid on prices, but how long can an increase in recycling offset structural demand growth from industrial uses like photovoltaics? What if the increasing risks surrounding the Treasury market trigger important capital flows towards gold? And what if a de minimis percentage of the capital flowing towards gold ultimately trickles down to the very small and illiquid silver market? This is when we could get an explosion. Bujon Capital On Silver

By paper gold assets, I am referring to ETFs like the SPDR Gold Shares ETF (GLD), the iShares Gold Trust ETF (IAU), the Sprott Physical Gold Trust (PHYS) or the abrdn Physical Gold Shares ETF (SGOL), among others. The same ETF providers have similar instruments with silver, including the iShares Silver Trust ETF (SLV), the Sprott Physical Silver Trust (PSLV) and the abrdn Physical Silver Shares (SIVR), among others. In terms of size and trading volume, the gold and silver ETF complex is relatively small compared to futures traded on the COMEX or over-the-counter trading with the London Bullion Market Association.

To give you an idea of scale, the SPDR Gold Shares ETF is the largest gold ETF with a market capitalization of ~$100 billion. As of the end of March 2025, GLD owned approximately 930 metric tons of gold, representing less than 10% of the ~8,500 metric tons stored in LBMA's London vaults. The same relationship is also observable with silver. In short, the real price-setting power in the paper gold complex sits in the hands of the COMEX in New York and the LBMA in London. It is not by coincidence that those two cities also form the financial powerhouses of the Western World.

In the coming weeks, I will try to gain a better understanding of the metal flows between the West and the East. After all, precious metals in Eastern countries, such as China and India, have been cultural symbols for thousands of years, so the general population is more familiar with the topic. In the Western world, the situation is quite different, and discussing this topic is even taboo in certain circles. In this context, should we be surprised by the massive amount of gold flowing from Western vaults to Eastern vaults?

What About Mining Equities?

After joining this platform in September 2024, I have written about ~30 different gold and silver miners, as well as a dozen royalty and streaming companies. In my mind, they are all derivatives of paper gold, which is in turn a derivative of physical gold. Within the universe of gold mining equities, junior exploration companies like Amex Exploration (AMX:CA) and Collective Mining (CNL:CA) would be at one extreme of the risk spectrum, while senior producers like Agnico-Eagle (AEM) and Newmont (NEM) would be on the other extreme. Small and intermediate producers that are well-capitalized would sit in the middle. Even for the highest-quality companies, gold mining remains a challenging business carrying a lot more risks compared to the metal itself. They can indeed provide life-changing gains during a bull market, but it is precisely why I view them as derivatives of paper gold. They are paper gold with a dose of leverage.

The minute we talk about equities, we implicitly talk about management teams acting as intermediaries. As minority shareholders, we do not necessarily share the same set of financial incentives as the people in executive positions or sitting on boards, and this is precisely the definition of 'Agency Risk'. The people running the show and the people providing the capital may not have the same financial goals.

For those wanting to avoid many of the risks associated with mining operations, royalty and streaming assets are an alternative. In general terms, royalty and streaming portfolios are more diversified, on top of being largely immune to rising operating costs during inflationary times. As an asset class, royalty and streaming companies are less risky than mining companies, but still more risky than paper gold, which is in turn more risky than physical gold.

We could go even further down on the risk curve by mentioning call or put options on mining companies. In this case, we are talking about derivatives based on a derivative of paper gold, which is in turn a derivative of the physical metal. This is how deep one can go within the financial system while remaining exposed to the same underlying asset: physical gold. Now, are there enough metals in the vault to cover all those paper contracts? That's the real question. What is it all worth if you cannot enforce your contracts and put your hands on your metal? If you cannot touch it, do you really own it?

To be clear, I am not trying to say that one financial instrument is better than the other, as it all depends on what you are personally trying to do. If your goal is to protect generational wealth, trading call options on gold miners is probably not the best idea. In reverse, if you are looking for liquidity and active trading, physical metals are probably not appropriate either.

Within all those gold and silver-related asset classes, we can see that the key battle is between the physical and the paper assets. Over the last few decades, with the rise of highly financialized societies, prices on global markets have been set by paper assets. Now that the reality on the ground has never been so divergent from the situation on paper, it is my personal opinion that we are about to witness a paradigm shift, a polarity shift. Every single ounce that is asked for delivery tightens the screw on the paper complex, and that is why a sudden lost of faith in fiat currencies could trigger something truly historic. Just imagine a failure to deliver on the COMEX or LBMA. As Thomas S. Kuhn wrote in his book 'The Structure of Scientific Revolutions':

If one paradigm is destined to win its fight, the number and strenght of the persuasive arguments in its favor will increase. More scientits will then be converted, and the exploration of the new paradigm will go on. Gradually the number of experiements, instruments, articles, and books based upon the paradigm will multiply.

It All Comes Down To Counterparty Risk

In previous articles, I discussed the risk of a panic on the U.S. Treasury market, leading to price-insensitive buyers rushing towards precious metals. In such a scenario, most of the capital in dollar terms would obviously flow towards gold. However, given the minuscule size of the silver market, this is where price inelasticity could translate into explosive returns.

With the U.S. federal debt approaching the $37 trillion mark, just think about the tsunami of capital that could be unleashed onto precious metals. With endless fiscal deficits and surging interest expenses, an increasing number of people are seeking to escape the rat race. Why would I save my hard-earned capital in fiat currencies when governments can print them at will, at no cost, and at the most opportune time for them? Bujon Capital On Iamgold

The key difference between physical and paper gold comes down to counterparty risk. In the event of serious liquidity problems in the U.S. Treasury market, counterparty risk would quickly become a top concern for investors. If your primary goal with precious metals is to protect yourself against counterparty risks, it would then be self-defeating to use paper gold as a financial tool. The rapid increase in gold and silver deliveries on the COMEX is a key indication that more and more institutional investors are worried about this precise risk.

Following the 2008 Global Financial Crisis, counterparty risks within the over-the-counter derivatives market became apparent. In an attempt to mitigate the issue, central clearing was introduced to multiple segments of the derivative market. In December 2016, the Bank for International Settlements published a note indicating that approximately 75% of derivatives on interest rates were now centrally cleared. Almost a decade later, I would guess that this number is probably above 90% now.

In other words, a serious dislocation on the treasury market would put tremendous pressure on the balance sheet of those central counterparties, including the Depository Trust & Clearing Corporation, with $99 trillion worth of securities in custody. What I am trying to express with the term 'serious dislocation' is an event in which long-term interest rates start to spike upward, similar to what we have witnessed on the night of April 9th, when 10-year yields surged by more than 25 bps. This is when the market stress associated with Trump's Liberation Day became serious, and it is no coincidence that a 90-day pause on most reciprocal tariffs was announced the next day. Another example would be the 'Lis Trust Moment' on U.K. Guits in the fall of 2022. Both of those events were contained quite rapidly, but can they represent a kind of window into the future? One thing is clear: the volatility on long-term interest rates is now to the upside, which is in sharp contrast to what the Western world experienced after 10-year yields in the United States peaked at 15.82% in September of 1981.

As discussed in great detail by David Rogers Webb in his book 'The Great Taking', the balance sheets of the central clearing entities sitting in the middle of today's financial system can absord just a few billions worth of losses, so it would not take much of an accident on the Treasury market to create a hole on the capital base of those firms. In case of insolvency, and given the leverage involved, what would happen to the trillions worth of securities in their custody? Would client assets be returned to their rightful owner or included in bankruptcy proceedings? If ever included in a bankruptcy process, where would the assets in custody rank in the recovery waterfall? This is when the difference between physical and paper becomes clear. With physical, there is no need to worry about whether the DTCC's balance sheet is properly capitalized or not.

As of March 31, 2023, the consolidated Total Shareholder's Equity was a tad over $3.5 billion (that's with a 'b'). Now realize that this is the entire capitalization underpinning the Central Security Depository and CCPs for the entire U.S. securities market and derivatives complex. The Great Taking, David Rogers Webb

Conclusion

Having a bullish stance on precious metals is one thing, but expressing this view with the appropriate financial instrument is something different. One example of basis risk is the significant underperformance of certain gold miners during what is already a solid bull market in gold prices. We can also think about political risks, as highlighted by the recent nationalization of five gold mines in Burkina Faso and the inability to renew mining licenses in Mali. In an inflationary environment, miners must also deal with upside volatility on operating costs, so higher gold prices do not necessarily translate into share price appreciation. What about other operational risks like the seismic events IAMGOLD had to deal with at Westwood, or the collapse of a heap leach pad at the Çöpler Mine owned and operated by SSR Mining (SSRM)? This kind of event is relatively rare, but you only need one to wipe you out, as it happened to Victoria Gold's shareholders last summer.

All the risks mentioned above are important, but they are generally well understood by investors. In my view, counterparty risks associated with central clearing counterparties are where the real blind spot is. This is a risk that is not well understood, and I have found very little content about it on this platform, which is why I decided to write this article.

Just a few days ago, mainstream media all around the world were discussing the potential closure of the Strait of Hormuz following a rapid escalation of geopolitical tensions in the Middle East. There is also ample media attention around the increasing tensions on both sides of the Taiwan Strait, one of the key shipping lanes for containerships. As simulated by the Bank of England last year, a sudden crystallization of geopolitical risks could ignite a crisis in the U.S. Treasury market.

At the end of the day, everyone owning financial assets like stocks and bonds is exposed to counterparty risks with central clearing parties. Is it right, is it wrong? To me, it is just a fact. Everyone owning paper assets is implicitly making a bet on the balance sheet of entities like the DTCC. How risky is such a bet? Again, everyone will have to answer this question for themselves. My goal is to ask hard questions that few people in this frothy market are thinking about.

Physical gold and silver are among the few assets without counterparty risks. This is why we are seeing record gold and silver deliveries on the COMEX. This is also why a loss of faith in the U.S. Treasury market could start a truly historic move. With silver prices still hovering around $35.00, I think we are still early in this worldwide game of musical chairs. The number of people still dancing is completely disproportionate relative to the number of chairs available.

By using the word 'chairs', I am referring to the limited supply of real assets without counterparty risks, relative to the unbounded money-printing capabilities of central banks. At the end of the day, the best way to protect oneself against the counterparty risks described in this article is to directly own tangible assets outside the financial system. In this regard, physical gold and silver fulfill a critical function, but precious metals are far from being the only option.

After more than three years in the woods, I am fully aware of how useless precious metals are when it comes to day-to-day living. We cannot eat metal, and coins are also useless when it is time to heat my yurt in the winter, when the temperature outside can drop to -40 °C. Given my personal lifestyle, my portfolio of real assets also includes enough food to last for at least a winter, and enough white birch and quaking aspen to last a few winters. Given how far I am from the closest town, having enough propane and gasoline is also important, and having a spare chainsaw is not a luxury either.

To me, all of those commodities are a lot more important than precious metals. Metals are useful to protect against excess liquidity; they are at the apex of my personal pyramid of needs. If the opposite were true, I could not last a single winter in the wild. Everyone will have to decide for themselves how deep inside the counterparty risk rabbit hole they want to go, but it is in this very process that the unique properties of precious metals as tools of financial insurance become apparent.

data.title: Teekay Corporation: Believe What Management Says
data.description:
Teekay (TK) management stated in the latest quarterly report that uncertain times lie ahead. It is time to believe them and listen to what they are saying.

Since the market tends to look out into the future and the market hates uncertainty, investors would be very wise to firmly evaluate what is being stated here within whatever framework they are using to decide to invest. It very much appears to me that statements like the above mean it's time to raise cash.

Since management also had few worries about the immediate future, that likely means there are a few months before whatever will happen unfolds. So, there is no need to "rush for the doors" as institutions are apt to do. But if you feel as I do that institutional panic is on the way, as is the case with the beginning of most downturns, then it is very wise to be in front of that panic and not wait for it.

Some buy and hold investors will hang on with the idea that they are in it "forever". I have known many people that never sell. When my uncle was around, he hated paying taxes. But he still left roughly $4 million to my cousin.

So, there is something to this buy and never sell strategy. David Dreman in his series of "Contrarian Investing Strategy" series of books covers the well-established literature researching holding periods. Evidently, the longer you hold, the more likely you are to come out ahead of your costs. But those studies do not include the time value of money as a rule. Even so, for some people, this is a satisfactory possibility.

The biggest risk of a buy and hold strategy is that investors have a tendency to lose their nerve either at market bottoms (which causes a big loss) or during the recovery when they can break even or have a small profit. This last issue often means that they miss out on most of the gains a recovery brings.

These are things to consider when management gives a warning like the one above. There is enough of those kinds of warnings that it is becoming clear that now is the time to make a cash raising strategy (if you do not have one already) as opposed to buying.

First Quarter Results

First quarter results were actually pretty decent. It would appear that there will be a good second quarter after that.

One thing about shipping is that the money tends to be made "all at one time". There is a likelihood that fiscal year 2024 contained the peak earnings period even if we did not know it at the time.

University of Michigan Survey

One of the more accurate measures of what lies ahead is the University of Michigan Survey of consumer sentiment. It actually took such a big dive that the result creates a "near certain" outcome that is not good for the future. The only thing about academic research is that the timing is uncertain.

This gives quite a bit of credence to the management warning that was first shown in this article. This article summarized some of the major intuitive deductions made by the people that conducted the survey. With an initial 2025 decline as large as the one shown above, there is not a whole lot of doubt as to "what comes next". It is just a matter of waiting until it happens.

But so many look at current government figures to dispute something like this without realizing the government figures are often history when they are reported. This is a future event.

Consumers often bring on recessions or worse by simply not spending because they are afraid. This is one case where fears turn into reality.

Atlanta Federal Reserve

Additionally, the Atlanta Federal Reserve feelings were summarized in an article by Forbes written by Erik Sherman On April 3, 2025. The fear there is that the GDP growth rating is deteriorating at such a speed that the rate of negative change may catch consumers by surprise.

This is yet another fairly reliable measurement that has a decent degree of accuracy.

Summary And Last Article

The last article had Teekay as a strong sell precisely because economic worries were appearing on the horizon. This company has a far better balance sheet than it had years ago when I first began coverage. As a result, it should survive whatever happens.

However, the market looks forward and it is possible that it may not like what it sees. Contrast this to the comments section in the last article about strong rates and how much they improved (to go with whatever point the commenter made). Even management has in their comments for the first quarter that rates should remain in good shape for the second quarter. The issue is the second half of the fiscal year and of course the next fiscal year.

With cyclical stocks, Mr. Market often looks ahead. Therefore, cyclical stocks often look dirt cheap (like they cannot go any lower) before "the bottom drops out" and the stock heads to values not thought possible.

The stock is up very roughly about 25% from the last article despite the strong sell. I continue to believe that this is the time to take profits and get ready for what comes next. With all the warnings from the reputable sources shown above, what comes next does not appear to be very pleasant. In fact, it brings to mind the old saying "cash is king".

I continue to rate this a strong sell for the reasons given in the article.

Risks

The biggest risk, as management noted, is the political uncertainty as to the effects of what may happen in the future. This is something beyond the control of management.

The other uncertainty is the timing of any cyclical downturn, its severity and its duration. This company is in good shape to survive such a situation. The question is if an investor wants to wait for the next pricing recovery as timing is always uncertain in this business. Shipping, in particular rates, can be very slow to recover from a recession (or something worse).

I personally do not view Teekay common stock as severely overpriced. But the market often has a period of "throw them all out" during a cyclical downturn that can scare an investor. If you hold the stock during the downturn, then be prepared to hold it until the next favorable time to sell arrives. Do not be one of those that gets out at breakeven or with a small profit at the start of a cyclical recovery. Such investors often miss the better part of the gains a recovery enables for the stock price.

data.title: Janus Henderson: Equity Market Correction Worries Dampen Value Appeal
data.description:
Introduction

Janus Henderson Group (JHG) reported first quarter 2025 results on May 01, 2025. In this note, I highlight key positive and negative issues that caught my eye whilst reviewing JHG’s 1Q25 materials and updating my financial model, and I take a look at JHG’s new partnership with Guardian Life Insurance Company of America.

I will also assess whether or not the incremental operational improvements that JHG has made over recent quarters, combined with a recovery in equity markets, provides enough valuation support to warrant an upgrade from the Hold rating that I published in April 2025.

1Q25 Positives

Below is a brief summary and discussion of the key positives that grabbed my attention whilst working through the JHG 1Q25 materials:

  • JHG reported positive net flows for 1Q25. The group has now delivered net flows in positive territory for four successive quarters. Net flows in the key US region have been positive for the last seven quarters. The turnaround in JHG’s net flows has been a little surprising, and given the relatively high level of redemptions (discussed below), I still would not be surprised to see occasional quarterly net outflows over the next year or two.

  • On April 08, 2025, JHG announced a new partnership with Guardian Life Insurance Company of America (Guardian). I will discuss the Guardian partnership further below, but the key positives to take away are a) JHG will be bringing on board $45bn of new AUM from around the end of 2Q25, and b) Guardian will commit up to $400m of seed capital for new Fixed Income product innovation. My initial thought was that this was a big win for JHG, but as discussed further below, the earnings upside for JHG may not be particularly significant, at least in the near-term.
  • JHG’s balance sheet is in good shape. The company announced an increased dividend of $0.40 per share, and a new buyback of up to $200m. After pausing buybacks in FY23, JHG has now completed six consecutive quarters of buyback activity.

1Q25 Negatives

Below is a brief summary and discussion of the key negatives that caught my eye whilst working through the JHG 1Q25 materials:

  • Although net flows were again positive in 1Q25, the level of redemptions was significantly higher than we have seen over the last couple of years. Another slightly negative issue on net flows is that the strength is coming mainly from active ETFs, which are likely to be relatively low management fee rate products.

  • On a benchmark relative view, 1-year performance numbers for Equities were very soft, with only 20% of AUM ahead of the benchmark. This is the worst 1-year Equities benchmark relative performance in the 26 quarters that I have been tracking JHG. The 1-year Equities numbers were somewhat better on a peer-relative basis, with 57% of AUM in the top two quartiles. Longer-term metrics for Equities, and also total AUM, remain solid.

  • Guidance for FY25E non-compensation expense growth relative to FY24 has increased, and is now stated as ‘at the higher end of mid-to-high-single digits’ on FY24 levels. The previous FY25E guidance given at 4Q24 referenced ‘mid-to-high-single digits’.
  • JHG had previously given guidance that the net management fee margin would increase in FY25E, mainly due to asset mix shift. However, with fee-rich Equities having had a weak first quarter, and the new $45bn Guardian Fixed Income portfolio coming on-stream at the end of 2Q25, JHG is now guiding to a decline in the FY25E net management fee margin. The full net management fee margin impact of the Guardian portfolio (which is best thought of as an asset mix shift) is expected to be -5bp to -6bp. I will look at this issue in closer detail below.

Guardian Partnership

Key elements of the new partnership with Guardian are summarized in the slide below:

A couple of important additional points are not mentioned in JHG’s slide above:

  • Guardian investment staff currently working on portfolios that will transfer to JHG will be offered the opportunity to join JHG’s Fixed Income team. I see this as a positive in terms of supporting an ongoing relationship with Guardian, however JHG is also taking on the risk of employing new staff that have not been through the group’s usual recruitment process.
  • JHG will issue Guardian with equity warrants and ‘other economic consideration’, which are intended to support a shared goal of accelerating growth and value creation. JHG has not provided details in regard to the equity warrants and other payments to Guardian; this lack of disclosure is somewhat disappointing, but it is possible that the numbers involved are very small in the context of JHG’s overall business.

The addition of $45bn in Fixed Income represents a material change in JHG’s AUM mix. Using the 1Q25 AUM as a starting point, the proportion of JHG’s AUM relating to Fixed Income will increase from ~24% to ~32%. As noted above, the full net management fee margin impact of this asset mix shift is expected to be -5bp to -6bp. In the table below, I provide an estimate of the net management fee margin impact of adding $45bn of Fixed Income to JHG’s AUM. Note that I have used the net management fee margin for each asset segment that JHG has previously disclosed for FY24.

The analysis above points to a net management fee margin impact of adding $45bn in Fixed Income of -2.3bp. The net management fee margin impact that JHG has guided to for the Guardian mandate is therefore more than double my estimated effect. The conclusion to draw from this analysis is that the Guardian mandate will generate a very low net management fee margin for JHG.

In fact, based on a quick calculation, to get to a net management fee margin impact of -5bp, the $45bn Guardian portfolio appears to be barely above break even at the net management fee margin level. If JHG is making such a very low management fee margin on the Guardian portfolio, why does the partnership make sense? JHG has highlighted other benefits that it hopes will be delivered:

  • Becoming a more significant player in terms of provision of investment services to the global insurance industry. JHG says that the partnership will make the group a top 15 unaffiliated insurance asset managers.
  • Up to $400m of seed capital for Fixed Income product innovation. I note that no detail has been provided regarding the timing of the seed capital flows, or the terms of this arrangement.
  • JHG and Guardian will co-develop multi-asset solution model portfolios for Guardian’s dually registered broker-dealer and registered investment advisor, Park Avenue Securities.
  • Organic growth in the Guardian mandate over time based on a flow-through of growth in Guardian’s business.

My conclusion at present is that the Guardian deal is likely to be a small incremental positive for JHG in the near-term. If JHG is able to capture benefits from the launch of new products that have been seeded by Guardian’s capital and gather further AUM via Park Avenue Securities, then it is possible that the partnership may deliver more material shareholder value over time.

Closing Remarks & Rating Update

Typically, the market will reward fund managers that are producing consistent positive net flows with a higher earnings multiple. If JHG can produce another couple of positive net flow quarters, I think the chance of an earnings multiple re-rating is quite high.

Given the sharp recovery in equity markets since the April 2025 post tariff announcement lows, there may be scope for JHG to upgrade FY25E guidance for the net management fee margin.

JHG is currently at ~$38.74 (market close June 27, 2025). At this level, my updated Base Case normalized valuation framework generates a P/E of ~10.8x. This is below my fair-value P/E benchmark for a fund manager of around 12x. Note that my valuation includes the positive mark-to-market effect of the gain in equity markets since the end of 1Q25 upon JHG’s AUM.

Given the sharp bounce in equity markets over the last couple of months, I am concerned that there is a prospect of significant share price weakness for JHG if equity markets correct to the downside. As mentioned in my previous JHG note, in volatile and unpredictable markets, I lack enthusiasm to take on the beta exposure that comes with investing in fund managers unless I see significant valuation upside.

For investors who feel optimistic about the outlook for equity markets, JHG may be worthy of consideration given that the stock is not expensive and that the operational performance of the company is solid. For me, with JHG currently trading only slightly below my fair value benchmark, I conclude this review with a Hold rating. My rating will likely prove to be too cautious if equity markets remain buoyant.

data.title: MDST: Get Steady High Income From This Midstream Energy ETF
data.description:
Energy midstream stocks offer an attractive option for US investors looking for steady high income. The midstream category includes pipelines, storage and processing facilities. Companies in this sector would typically generate stable, fee-based cash flows from long term contracts.

Unlike exploration and production companies, midstream companies are much less exposed to volatile fluctuations in crude oil and natural gas prices which we experienced lately because of the Israel-Iran conflict. Their fees are based on the volume of energy transported or stored, and not on the commodity price.

Because energy infrastructure is so critical to the economy, midstream investments tend to be a defensive investment and more resilient when there are economic uncertainties. But they are also heavily regulated by federal, state and local governments. Changes in regulation like stricter pipeline permitting, or scrutiny from environmental groups can affect the cost of operations and profits.

One of the potential negatives to midstream investing can be tax complexity. Many midstream companies are structured as Master Limited Partnerships (or MLPs). MLPs offer some tax advantages at the corporate level (e.g. no corporate income tax). But they can significantly complicate the tax return of an individual investor for two reasons:

  1. They generate a Schedule K-1 For tax filing: This is much morecomplicated than the standard 1099-DIV and usually arrives later in the tax season. In some cases, it can also require tax filings in multiple states.

  2. UBTI: This stands for Unrelated Business Taxable Income. This can jeopardize the tax advantages in an IRA or 401K when UBTI is more than $1,000.

There are several ways to own MLPs. In this article, I discuss the Westwood Salient Enhanced Midstream Income ETF (NYSE:MDST) which is fairly new with an inception date of April 8, 2024. It has been steadily growing and recently it surpassed $100 million in assets under management. The tax treatment for the fund is 1099 (No K-1s).

The ETF uses an active fundamental-driven strategy and tries to provide investors with high steady monthly distributions enhanced by selling options. They try to deliver 10-20% less volatility than the Alerian Midstream Index. The portfolio is somewhat concentrated with about 15-25 holdings, with a 1-2% tracking error to the Alerian Midstream Index.

Options Strategy

The Fund uses a disciplined single-stock options strategy, writing call options that average are 4 to 5% out-of-the-money, sold on a monthly recurring basis.

MDST offers a convenient way for an income investor to invest in the midstream sector You have to pay a management fee of 0.80%., but do gain some advantages:

  • You receive one Form 1099 per shareholder instead of multiple K-1 forms.

  • The MDST options overlay program which generates higher income that has recently been return of capital.

  • MDST shares do not generate unrelated business taxes.

  • MDST manages to pay a high monthly distribution without the use of leverage.

  • The Fund uses tax-loss swap trades to generate tax losses where possible.

  • The ETF wrapper may be more tax efficient compared to other investment vehicles.

The asset structure allocation of the Fund as of March 31, 2025 was 24.33% MLP and 76.39% C-Corp. This allocation is for long only securities and does not include cash or options.

Because the MLP allocation is below 25%, MDST can be structured as an RIC or registered investment company. Most funds registered under the Investment Company Act of 1940 (often called ’40 Act Funds) are RICs. RICs are considered pass-through entities and aren’t subject to corporate income taxes.

Seeking Alpha Quant ETF Ratings

MDST is a fairly new ETF, and the Seeking Alpha ETF quant model gives it a 2.80 or Hold rating. The weaker areas are expenses and liquidity.
