Summary

MDST is not the most liquid ETF. The bid-asked spread is usually around five cents, but can get higher during slow periods. Limit orders should be used on purchases or sales, and larger orders should be entered near the close of the day when liquidity is usually greater.

MDST provides a reasonable way for less active income-oriented investors to gain access to the midstream sector. Because recent distributions have been 100% ROC, it can be a good holding in a retirement account or a taxable account. For liquidity reasons, I would look to gradually scale into MDST over a period of time using multiple small limit orders.

data.title: ZAG:CA: Offering Up Investment Grade Bonds For Your Palette
data.description:
All values are in CAD unless noted otherwise.

We just wrote about the BMO Low Volatility Canadian Equity ETF (ZLB:CA). While we stayed out, we were impressed with the management and performance of the fund. We agreed that it would make a great addition to a portfolio, for a section of investors. Today, we shift focus from equity to fixed income and tackle another fund from the same family, the BMO Aggregate Bond Index ETF (ZAG:CA). This fund holds investment grade bonds.

Formed in 2010, this $11 billion fund comprises federal, provincial and corporate bonds. The government portion makes up three quarters of the portfolio, with federal bonds leading the pack.

The combination of high-level investment grade and primarily government issuances, gives this fund an extremely low-risk profile from a credit standpoint. ZAG:CA predominantly invests in bonds with maturity of greater than one year, with the weighted average maturity of the portfolio being 9.81 years. At the individual holding level, the term to maturity ranges from under five years to thirty plus years.

While the above provides information on the term to maturity of the holdings, investors fall back on the duration data to inform themselves on the interest rate sensitivity of the portfolio. The weighted average duration of the portfolio is a moderate 7.13 years.

Portfolio duration, in broad terms, means the extent to which a portfolio value will change in response to the rise or fall in interest rates. The relationship between the two is inverse and the 7.13 is in percentage points. So if for instance, the comparable risk-free rate were to suddenly decline by 100 basis points, the ZAG portfolio will appreciate by around 7%. We used a rate decline to illustrate because there is no talk or thought of a rate hike at this time. After a slew of rate cuts by the Bank of Canada since mid-2024, the rates were held steady this month.

The uncertainties regarding the trade negotiations and tariffs, along with sticky core inflation put a pause in the step of the decision makers. This is after a fast-paced easing from 5% to 2.75% over the last year.

The Bank of Canada held its benchmark interest rate unchanged at 2.75% in its June 2025 decision, as expected by half of the market, to mark the second hold following 2.25 percentage points of cuts in seven consecutive decisions. The governing council noted that the ongoing increase and decrease of various US tariffs, coupled with highly uncertain outcomes of bilateral trade negotiations and tariff rates remaining well above their levels at the beginning of 2025, placed downside risks on growth and lifted inflation expectations, warranting caution regarding the continuation of monetary easing.

Source: Trading Economics

While they cautioned about the continuation of easing, the consensus is that there will be further easing this year, the only question is how much. So there is no conventional threat to the ZAG:CA portfolio from duration risk at this point. Of course, the unconventional risk remains that the curve steepens dramatically with long-term rates rising, even if cuts are executed at the front end. We have actually seen this in the US as longer-term rates rose after the Federal Reserve cuts. So keep that in mind. That said, a 7-year duration shouldn't make you sweat that much.

Collectively, the holdings have a weighted average coupon of 3.40%, while the weighted average yield to maturity [YTM] is 3.55%. The YTM broadly signifies what this ETF would make if the bonds were held to maturity from this point onwards. ZAG:CA has annual expenses 0.09%, the bulk (although it feels wrong to apply "bulk" to this number) of which is from management fees.

With a YTM of 3.55% and expenses of 0.09%, the ETF has a ballpark 3.46% net income available to distribute. ZAG:CA has been distributing 4 cents on a monthly basis since 2019. According to the fund website, ZAG:CA caters to investors looking for consistent income. The stable distribution certainly meets that criteria. As to the magnitude of the income, the annual payout of 48 cents, when applied to the current price of $13.82, gets us to a yield of 3.47%. ETFs typically pay out what they earn, and this ETF is no different.

Performance

ZAG:CA is a passive ETF, which means that it attempts to replicate the performance of an index. The index in question is the FTSE Canada Universe Bond Index. The proportion and selection methodology are briefly explained in the annual Management Report of Fund Performance [MRFP].

The investment strategy of the ETF is to invest in and hold the constituent securities of the Index in the same proportion as they are reflected in the Index. The Manager may also use a sampling methodology in selecting investments for the ETF.

Source: Annual MRFP

The ETF changed its beacon aka index in 2020. Previously it held the incumbents of the FTSE Canada UniverseXM Bond Index. Nonetheless, the performance comparison with the current index is comparable, with most of the difference appearing to be from the expenses (the index has none).

For the time periods where the performance has a variance more or less than what could be attributed to the annual expenses of the fund, it lucked out (or not) with its sampling and timing.

Verdict

The rate cuts over the last year boosted this ETF's returns to over 7%. The longer-term returns are more in line with what one would expect from an investment grade fund, holding primarily government bonds to boot. While there is nothing wrong with this fund at the moment, we prefer making our fixed income picks with independent analysis.

We find gems in the rough (read non-investment grade) from issuers like Diversified Royalty Corp (DIV:CA) and StorageVault Canada Inc. (SVI:CA). Then we rinse and repeat. It is not hard to find 6%-9% yields and even better annualized returns, where the risks are minimal. However, just like ZLB:CA, this moderate duration, fixed income offering from BMO will make a good addition to the portfolio of those that want the consistency afforded by owning an ETF, at the price of lower (albeit still positive) returns.

Please note that this is not financial advice. It may seem like it, sound like it, but surprisingly, it is not. Investors are expected to do their own due diligence and consult a professional who knows their objectives and constraints. Most of our articles are not written for "buy and hold" investors. Our articles are definitely not written for investors who ignore capital preservation while focusing only on yield.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Seaboard: With A Stable Net Income, Low Debt, And Trading Below Book Value, Is It Undervalued?
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I grant Seaboard Corporation (NYSE:SEB) a Buy rating. I am of the opinion that the company is significantly undervalued. To start with, the company has a solid financial position. For instance, it can pay its long-term debt using its cash and cash equivalents, and short-term investments. Its current assets exceed its current liabilities. In other words, the risk of bankruptcy is very low. Furthermore, the company has reported positive net income in eight of the last nine years, indicating its ability to generate profits. Additionally, the capital expenditures ((capex)) have been at least 1.5 times higher than the depreciation and amortization ((D&A)) since 2019. This fact indicates that the company has allocated significant financial resources to expanding its operations.

Surprisingly, Seaboard trades with a considerable discount. The price-to-book and EV/EBITDA ratios are close to their historical 10-year low values. In addition, the relative valuation technique applied to the price-to-book ratio suggests that the company presents an undervaluation of 75.76%. Meanwhile, the same technique applied to the EV/EBITDA indicates that Seaboard is undervalued by 46.40%.

In my opinion, the considerable undervaluation is due to the problems that the U.S. pork market faces. The Pork Segment of Seaboard reported operating losses in 2022, 2023, and Q1 2025. To provide context, this segment led Seaboard's operating income until 2021. The U.S. pork market does not exhibit the rapid growth rate that it exhibited up to 2020. Since that year, the U.S. pork production has remained relatively stable. However, commodities are cyclicals. I think the pork market could experience a favorable market outlook in the upcoming years. When pondering all the positive and negative aspects of Seaboard, I still consider the company to be an interesting investment opportunity.

The Company

Seaboard is a conglomerate with international operations headquartered in Merriam, Kansas, U.S. The company is engaged in hog production, pork processing, commodity trading, grain processing, cargo shipping services, electric power generation, sugar production, and alcohol production.

In 2024, the Commodity Trading and Milling ((CT&M)) Segment accounted for 52% of the consolidated net sales, followed by the Pork Segment with 23%, the Marine Segment with 16%, the Fuel Segment with 6%, and the Power Segment with 3%, as depicted in the first chart below. Meanwhile, that year, 43% of Seaboard's net sales came from the Caribbean, Central, and South America; 27% from Africa; 23% from the U.S.; and the remaining percentage from other countries, as depicted in the second chart below.

From a financial perspective, the company has a strong position. When excluding 2018, it has reported positive net income since 2016, which reflects its stability, as shown in the left chart below. In addition, the company has invested significant resources in expanding its operations. Since 2019, its capex has been at least 1.5 times higher than its D&A, as shown in the right chart below. Due to these investments, the revenues increased by 69.18% in 2016 and 2024. The company achieved this result despite the fact that its revenues have declined since 2022.

Competitive Advantage

I think that Seaboard has many competitive advantages. The size of its operation is one of those advantages. Seaboard processes over 6 million hogs annually. In fact, the company is the third-largest pork producer in the U.S. In 2024, 96% of the hogs processed in its plant were raised by the company. Consequently, the company does not purchase significant quantities of hogs from other resellers or growers, which could increase its costs.

The trading activity is the second competitive advantage. The company buys agricultural commodities directly from farmers and other market players. Raw materials are the main cost of raising hogs and turkeys. Consequently, it can reduce the cost of its raw materials in the pork segment, since it does not have to buy from intermediaries such as Bunge (BG) and Archer-Daniels-Midland (ADM). Additionally, Seaboard charters vessels to facilitate its trading activities and export its pork abroad.

Stock Price and Valuation Ratios

The following chart indicates that Seaboard's stock price is approaching its 10-year low. However, it also shows that the revenues have increased over a 10-year period. I think this is a sign of undervaluation.

To shed light on whether Seaboard is undervalued or not, the following table shows the price-to-book ratios of Seaboard, BG, ADM, Smithfield Foods (SFD), and Tyson Foods (TSN). BG and ADM are competitors of Seaboard in the CT&M Segment. Meanwhile, SFD and TSN compete with Seaboard in the pork business. Returning to the analysis, Seaboard has the lowest price-to-book ratio, at 0.59, which is considerably lower than the ratio reported by TSN ((the second lowest value)). To provide context, the median value of this ratio is 1.06.

When implementing the relative valuation technique, applied to the price-to-book ratio, using the median as the target ratio, Seaboard's stock price per share is $5,179.63. This value suggests that the company is undervalued by 75.76%, as the current stock price is $2,947.

The EV/EBITDA ratio also suggests that Seaboard is undervalued. As illustrated in the chart below, Seaboard's EV/EBITDA ratio is close to its historical 10-year low. The most recent value is 6.5. Compared to its peers, Seaboard has the lowest value. In addition, this ratio is lower than the median among its peers, which is 7.89. To provide a reference of Seaborn's possible undervaluation, I implemented the relative valuation. With the median as a target ratio, the fair value per share of Seaborn is $4,314.47. At this value, the company is undervalued by 46.40%. Both valuation methods point out that the company is undervalued.

Debt

In the previous section, I calculated the fair value of Seaboard. The two relative valuation methods pointed out that the company is undervalued. However, I did not analyze the debt structure of Seaboard. Sometimes companies that are undervalued tend to have high debt levels. Consequently, the undervaluation is due to the high leverage. However, Seaboard has low debt levels.

In Q1 2025, the company reported short-term investments of $1,015 million, and cash and cash equivalents of $87 million. With these resources, the company could pay its long-term debt of $987 million. During the same period, the company reported $3,489 million in current assets, while its current liabilities totaled $1,384 million. Therefore, these values indicate that the company has resources to pay its short-term liabilities.

Seaboard even has a better debt position than most of its peers, as depicted in the table below. It has a 36.75% debt-to-equity ratio, while the median of this ratio is 48.93%. In addition, its current ratio is higher than the median value. Surprisingly, the company is undervalued despite having a low debt position.

Pork Segment

In my opinion, the Pork Segment has been the primary factor in Seaboard's stock decline. This segment led the operating income from 2019 to 2021, as shown in the following chart. However, since 2022, it has reported losses or a small positive operating margin. In the first quarter of 2025, the Pork Segment reported an operating loss of $31 million. This poor performance has contributed to a decline in Seaboard's stock price. The company indicates that the pork market has been affected by high operating costs.

Pork Market

The trade tension between the U.S. and China did not start with the second mandate of U.S. President Donald Trump. For instance, in 2018, China imposed tariffs on American pork products. The trade dispute has affected the U.S. pork exports. Under the second administration of U.S. President Donald Trump, trade tensions between the two countries have increased. Due to this reason, U.S. pork meat exports to China face a tariff rate of 57%. China is the world's largest consumer of pork.

The following chart indicates that the U.S. pork exports reached their peak in 2020. To provide context, in 2024, the exports dropped by 2.26% compared to 2020. In addition, the June 2025 WASDE report projected that U.S. pork exports for 2025 and 2026 are expected to decline compared to 2024. Consequently, the outlook is not favorable.

To gain further insight into pork production, the following chart reveals that the U.S. pork meat production is expected to experience a slight increase in 2025 and 2026. In addition, the chart reveals that pork consumption per capita in the U.S. has decreased since 2019. Meanwhile, for 2025 and 2026, the pork consumption per capita is expected to experience a slight recovery.

Commodity Cycles

Commodities are cyclicals. Periods of high prices are characterized by low production levels. Subsequently, a period of low prices comes accompanied by an oversupply. This phenomenon is known as the boom-bust cycle in commodities.

In a 2023 report of the National Pork Producers Council, the American institution reviewed the U.S. pork industry of that year. In the article, the institution said:

As the industry emerges from the worst period of financial losses in its history, issues like labor shortages, foreign animal disease preparedness, market access, and occurrences and additional threats of state-by-state regulations dictating hog production practices and restricting pork sales have become increasingly critical to address.

From the quote, it appears that the pork market is not experiencing a boom. As commented on in the Pork Segment section. Seaboard has reported operating losses in the Pork Segment in 2022, 2023, and Q1 2025. It is not the only company with negative results. For instance, in 2023, a well-established hog farm in Iowa defaulted on over $38.9 million in debt. In my opinion, the pork market is draining the oversupply. I think the U.S. pork market will experience a positive outlook in the upcoming years, as commodities are cyclicals. Consequently, I think this is the opportune moment to invest in companies involved in the pork market, to capitalize on an eventual improvement in their market outlook.

Investors should take into consideration that the current headwinds may persist for several years. As such, Seaboard's stock prices could experience a further decline ((a risk factor)).

Forward Curve

The revenues of Seaboard are correlated to the pork and agricultural commodity prices. Due to this fact, I use the forward curve of lean hogs, corn, and soybeans to analyze the market expectations for these commodities. The forward curve shows the futures prices perspectives of producers, farmers, and speculators, at which they buy or sell futures derivatives of these commodities.

The following chart shows the lean hog forward curve, which serves as the reference for the pork market. The curve shows a bearish outlook. For example, the future contract of July 2025 quotes at 112.33 U.S. cents per pound. Meanwhile, the contract of December 2026 trades at 73.25 U.S. cents per pound. In other words, the lean hog price is expected to decline by 34.79% between now and December 2026, a significant decline. The lean hog future contract of December 2026 will decrease below the lean hog price average of 80.91 U.S. cents and 84.84 U.S. cents in 2023 and 2024, respectively. As such, this drop is a factor of risk.

Moving to the corn market, in 2023 and 2024, the corn spot price in Chicago averaged 564.76 U.S. cents per bushel and 428.28 U.S. cents per bushel, respectively. These two averages serve as a point of reference. Meanwhile, the forward curve suggests that the market expects the price of this commodity to rise from 409.5 U.S. cents now to 474.75 U.S. cents by July 2027, as illustrated in the chart below. The curve points out a more favorable outlook for corn prices compared to 2024. However, the forward curve suggests that corn prices will not reach the levels observed in 2023.

The soybean prices in Chicago averaged 1,396.17 U.S. cents per bushel in 2023, declining this average to 1,105.99 U.S. cents per bushel in 2024. On the other hand, the soybean forward curve suggests that the soybean prices will experience a slight increase in the upcoming years, as depicted in the following chart. The current price is 1,016.25 U.S. cents per bushel, and it is expected to reach around 1,065.25 U.S. cents per bushel in July 2026. However, this expected increase is below the average soybean price of 2024.

Peer Analysis

Up to this point, I have compared Seaboard to other companies in aspects such as valuation ratios and debt ratios. However, I have not analyzed other financial metrics. As such, this section conducts a peer analysis among Seaboard, BG, ADM, SFD, TSN, and Hormel Foods (HRL). BG and ADM compete with Seaboard in the trading business. SFD, TSN, and Horel Foods raise hogs or manufacture products derived from pork meat. The following table displays the ratios to be analyzed.

Seaboard is a company that is not on the spot. It was only covered by one Seeking Alpha analyst, suggesting that it could be overlooked by investors. On the other hand, in the last year, the company reported a 7.39% drop in its stock price. Meanwhile, over a 5-year period, the company experienced a 1.40% decline in its stock price. The previous two figures have similar values to the median stock prices decline in the peer group.

In the revenue growth metrics, Seaboard achieved slightly better values than those reported by its peers. To provide context, it achieved a 5.74% compound annual growth rate (OTC:CAGR) in its revenues over a 5-year period. In addition, in the last year, the company's EBITDA reported a 117.62% growth, considerably higher than the median of this ratio.

Moving to profit margin ratios, Seaboard reported a 6.84% margin, slightly lower than the median. The EBITDA margin of Seaboard is also lower than the median of its peers. Finally, the negative aspect is that the company reported a 2.12% return on equity, the lowest value.

Overall, I consider Seaboard to be in a better position than the median of its peers. Its stock price declined less than the median drop of its peers. Additionally, the company reported higher 5-year revenue growth compared to its peers. Seaboard lags behind only in the profit margin ratios. However, when considering all the ratios, I think that the company is in a good position.

Conclusion

In light of the analysis presented in this article, I consider Seaboard to be an excellent investment opportunity. The company has low debt levels. It has considerable liquid assets that can be used to pay its long-term debt and current liabilities. In addition, the company has reported positive net income in eight of the last nine years, indicating that it generates profit. These figures suggest that the risk of bankruptcy is low.

However, the company trades with a considerable discount compared to its peers. For instance, its price-to-book ratio is 0.59. At this level, the relative valuation technique suggests that the company is undervalued by 75.76%. In addition, Seaboard has an EV/EBIDTA of 6.50, the lowest value among its peers. The relative valuation applied to this ratio points out that the company is undervalued by 46.40%.

In my view, Seaboard's stock price is close to its historical low due to its Pork Segment. This segment contributed significantly to the company's operating income up to 2021. However, in 2022, 2023, and Q1 2025, it reported operating losses. Due to the challenging times this sector faces, Seaboard's stock price trades at a considerable discount. In fact, a report from the National Pork Producers Council said that the pork industry faced the worst period of financial losses in its history. Nonetheless, commodities are cyclicals. I think that this market will experience better prices due to the boom-and-bust cycle in the commodities.

For this year, hog prices are expected to decline to the levels observed in 2023 and 2024. Meanwhile, corn and soybean prices are expected to range from a small decline to a slight gain in the upcoming years. In addition, the U.S. pork production is expected to experience a small increase in 2025 and 2026. When taking all of these factors into consideration, I grant Seaboard a Buy rating without hesitation.

data.title: lululemon athletica: Growth Concerns Meet Low Valuations, I'm In
data.description:
Introduction

Growing EPS at a CAGR of 23% over the last ten years, lululemon athletica Inc. (NASDAQ:LULU) (NEOE:LULU:CA) (TSX:LLL:CA) earns the status of a true compounder. Recently, however, growth has taken a toll and slowed down drastically, with most recent financials and guidance indicating revenue growth at just 7% and 6%, respectively. The market has not taken set developments well and sold off the stock, drastically decreasing its valuation. From its ATH, the stock price has dropped 55%, erasing any gains made over the last five and a half years.

The key question now emerges whether LULU is a Buy today, which my answer to remains yes, since as with many great businesses, you will never get to buy them at a cheap price in the absence of temporary issues. I view Lululemon as a strong brand with solid growth prospects. I am not saying they will reach growth rates of 20%+ again, but for my thesis to work out, that is not necessary. What I am betting on is them growing in the high-single digits from 2025 onwards and continuing to expand their operating leverage by keeping costs down. Add share repurchases, and this will likely already suffice to achieve market-beating returns.

Why do I think so?

Q1 FY 2025 earnings were not horrible but did show heavy signs of slowing growth, as total revenues increased just 7% YoY. International revenues are up 19% (and China up 21%), suggesting further potential for global expansion.

When it comes to profitability, gross profit actually rose 8% (1% more than revenue), but net income dropped 2% (EPS rose 2% due to a lower share count), indicating cost pressures beyond material and sourcing.

The company guides for 7.5% of revenue growth in Q2 FY2025 and just 6% for FY2025, both at the midpoint. At $14.68 EPS is expected to remain flat, which does not include the effect of share repurchases, meaning we could very well see a beat in EPS. In 2026, EPS growth is expected at 7.1% YoY, if we are to trust the analysts.

Looking at historical revenue growth rates as depicted below, it instantly becomes noticeable that they only had one quarter of shrinking revenue since 2009, namely in early 2020 when Covid hit. This incredible growth history did not come along with perfect predictability, though, as in 2014, the situation was quite similar to today's - revenue growth rates spiked a few years before (2010) and then slowly faded off, resulting in a three-quarter rebound streak until dropping a little bit again and then settling at around 17% growth. Today, we are seeing the slow fade off from the 2021 Covid revenue growth explosion, the three quarters of increasing revenue growth and the recent one slightly lower. Acknowledging that LULU has been through such hardships before makes me more confident in their operating ability to overcome today's situation.

Sure, one might object that today's circumstances bear far greater challenges, such as tariffs and weakening consumer spending. However, trade deals have apparently already been made with China, are close to finishing with Europe and India, which should boost momentum on decreasing global tariff rates.

On TTM P/E and EV/EBITDA ratios, LULU has never changed hands more cheaply other than during the GFC, now trading at 15.9X and 8.9X, respectively. For me, this instantly sparks opportunity.

They also offer new investors an FCF yield of almost 5%, which again has only been higher during the GFC, where it was at 5.5%. This could be an indication of a drop in FCF already being priced in. At the very least, the market is not expecting growth rates in the double digits here anymore. That means that if LULU is able to deliver those, they are highly likely to experience multiple expansion. But as mentioned before, this is not my investment case.

Therefore, assuming LULU does keep growing but at lower rates, I think they could still be able to grow their EPS at around 10% for two reasons. First, they can use their operating leverage by keeping costs under control. The amount of people they employ just had its lowest YoY growth rate since 2009, at just 2.6%.

Further, costs such as COGS, SG&A, and D&A are also slowing down in growth. Personally, I would like to see SG&A as well as D&A shrink a little bit faster and only grow slower than revenues, which is currently only the case for COGS.

Second, they are highly FCF positive, do not pay a dividend and instead use their cash flows to buy back shares. In the last 12 months, this reduced the average count of shares outstanding by more than 4% and at such depressed prices, they are theoretically able to reduce the latter by 4% again, using the remaining $1.1B authorized, further boosting EPS.

What I do not like, however, is the growth of inventories and inventory turnover, which has fallen below a value of 3 in a continued downtrend. It is still broadly in line with competitors such as NIKE, Inc. (NKE) and On Holding (ONON), but should not continue its downtrend. Inventories of fashion brands tend to be very seasonal and susceptible to newly emerging trends. I get that inventories naturally need to scale up as part of their international expansion. Still, this trend has to be monitored, as a worsening can be an early sign of a continued worsening of revenue growth and margins due to markdowns.

Monthly technicals suggest oversold status for LULU, with RSI at just 40. Historically, they have often bottomed out at such levels. Further, monthly volume is the highest it has been in the last 8 years, which could also indicate a near bottom.

Risks

Lululemon faces several downside risks. Inventory levels remain elevated relative to historical norms, which increases the possibility of markdowns. The most obvious risk in my view is current trends in revenue deceleration simply continuing, leading to very weak EPS growth, or even shrinking EPS. Such a scenario is not priced in at the moment and would in turn lead to multiple compression. Altogether, this could lose new investors a lot of capital. For example, if annual EPS growth was at -2% for the next 3 years and the P/E ratio drops to 10X, 2027 stock price could see $137.8, resulting in a loss of 40%.

Conclusion

At current levels, Lululemon offers a compelling risk/reward setup. Their valuation already represents much of the revenue growth slowdown, trading at historically low multiples. I do view growth to be likely to bottom out in the mid-to-high single digits. Combined with rich buybacks and the stock could become an out performer. If LULU can return to double-digit growth over time (which isn't my investment case but remains possible) the upside from stronger EPS combined with multiple expansion would be substantial. That optionality adds attractive convexity to this investment. Given the quality of LULU's brand, their international potential and strong technicals, I believe some exposure at current prices is beneficial.

data.title: MID: Growth ETF With An ESG Approach
data.description:
MID strategy

American Century Mid Cap Growth Impact ETF (NYSEARCA:MID) is an actively managed ETF launched on 07/13/2020 with an objective of long-term capital growth. It has a portfolio of 40 stocks, a 30-day SEC yield of 0.15% and an expense ratio of 0.45%.

As described in the prospectus by American Century, eligible companies must align with at least one of the United Nations Sustainable Development Goals (SDG), meaning the fund has a loose environmental, social, and governance (“ESG”) approach. Then, portfolio managers select

…companies with attractive returns on invested capital that are demonstrating or expected to demonstrate long-term business improvement. Analytical indicators helping to identify or forecast signs of business improvement could include accelerating earnings or revenue growth rates, increasing cash flows, or other indications of the relative strength of a company’s business.

Like for most active ETFs, the strategy description offers a lot of flexibility to the fund’s managers and little transparency to investors. Additionally, MID doesn’t disclose its real holdings on a daily basis, but publishes a proxy portfolio supposed to track daily returns. The fund’s turnover rate was 68% in the most recent fiscal year. MID takes the Russell Midcap® Growth Index as a benchmark, but this article will use as a primary benchmark the S&P Mid-Cap 400 index, represented by iShares Core S&P Mid-Cap ETF (IJH).

Portfolio

MID is almost exclusively invested in US companies (96% of asset value), with a focus on information technology (28%) and significant exposure in consumer discretionary (20%), industrials (19%) and healthcare (15%). Other sectors weigh no more than 8%. Compared to the benchmark, MID mostly overweights technology, consumer discretionary and healthcare, while it significantly downplays financials, consumer staples and ignores real estate and communication.

The portfolio is quite concentrated, but risks related to individual companies are moderate. Indeed, the top 10 holdings of the proxy portfolio, listed in the next table, represent 39% of asset value and the heaviest position weighs 4.12%.

data.title: Gravity's Global Rollout Turns A Single Franchise Into A Deep Value Play
data.description:
Intro

Gravity’s (NASDAQ:GRVY) first-quarter print showed that the Ragnarok engine is still humming. Revenue rose to KRW 137.5 billion, or US$93.2 million, up 6% sequentially and 14.8% year-on-year as two new mobile launches offset normal aging in legacy titles. Operating profit advanced 55% quarter on quarter to KRW 24.7 billion, lifting margin back above 18% despite a heavier royalty mix, while net profit of KRW 22.0 billion translated to roughly US$2.15 of quarterly EPS on the tiny 6.95 million-share base. The beat matters because the stock still trades on single-digit earnings, barely above net cash that already covers more than 90% of the share price. With a refreshed pipeline rolling out across three continents and a balance sheet that looks more like a bank than a game studio, Gravity offers rare asymmetry: investors are paying little more than cash for an IP platform that keeps compounding.

Thesis

Gravity’s long-term runway rests on the flywheel of Ragnarok IP, a portfolio that continually refreshes itself across PC, mobile and now console, then franchises outward into animation and consumer products. Management’s co-development and licensing model lets the company scale in markets from Southeast Asia to Latin America without large fixed costs, so incremental launches drop quickly to the bottom line. New pipeline entries such as Ragnarok Origin 2, AI-infused casual titles and blockchain experiments extend the brand to fresh demographics, while Gravity’s publishing arm adds a second growth lever by distributing third-party hits. The combination of net cash, no debt, and the strategic umbrella of majority parent GungHo positions Gravity to compound earnings as a lean, IP-rich platform in the global games' economy. When that strategy meets a valuation of 7–8 times trailing earnings and 0.4-times EBITDA, the bull case writes itself.

Ragnarok Flywheel

What keeps Ragnarok relevant two decades after launch is an always-on schedule of spin-offs tuned to distinct regional tastes. Q1’s lift was driven by Ragnarok M: Classic in Southeast Asia and Ragnarok Idle Adventure Plus in the global English market. Each title reuses core art and lore but adapts mechanics and monetization, allowing Gravity to amortize R&D across multiple SKUs. Because most development is outsourced to specialist studios, Gravity’s in-house team remains small, holding SG&A to 15.9% of sales last quarter even with marketing for fresh launches. High-margin licensing revenue from China and Japan further juices returns: royalty streams carry 80%-plus gross margin, so every new territory turns the flywheel faster.

A second spoke is brand extension. The company opened its first “Ragnarok Golf Monsters” indoor screen-golf venue in Taipei during Q1, testing whether mascot characters can convert into experiential retail. Early foot-traffic data are anecdotal, yet the concept underscores how Gravity thinks about lifetime customer value: deepen emotional engagement, then monetize outside the core game loop. If the experiment scales, consumer-products revenue, now just 2% of the mix, could grow without diluting digital focus.

Pipeline Momentum

Looking ahead, at least nine Ragnarok titles are slated for multi-region release over the next twelve months, including Ragnarok X: Next Generation for Western consoles, Ragnarok Crush (puzzle tower-defense) and Ragnarok Landverse, a blockchain PC entry targeting Latin America. The cadence matters because mobile game cohorts typically decay 30-40% a year; constant new drops keep aggregate revenue flat to up despite individual drags. Management’s guidance, embedded in the investor deck, calls for mid-teens top-line growth for full-year 2025, a figure now de-risked by the Q1 beat.

Crucially, Gravity is diversifying beyond its own IP. Q1 saw console releases of Twilight Monk and Snow Brothers 2 Special, plus the Japanese launch of JLPGA Heroine Collection. Third-party publishing gives Gravity a wider funnel for content while capping downside through revenue-share deals. If only two out of ten external titles hit, the model still clears cost of capital given a 30% royalty take and near-zero inventory risk.

Global Publishing Leverage

Gravity’s subsidiary network now spans 17 offices, the latest being Gravity Game Unite in Malaysia, opened in March. These micro-hubs localize events, community management and payment rails without duplicating headquarters functions, lowering effective customer-acquisition cost. The strategy shines in emerging markets where distribution can be fragmented: direct service of Ragnarok Online in Latin America kicks off this quarter, replacing a third-party operator and instantly improving margin capture.

Parent company GungHo, which owns roughly 59% of Gravity, adds another layer of leverage by sharing technology and IP pipelines. That backing reduces financing risk and provides bargaining power when negotiating with platform holders from Apple to Tencent. For minority investors, the structure is a mixed blessing, but it does ensure strategic patience and access to GungHo’s Japan user base, historically one of Ragnarok’s most profitable regions.

Financials

Revenue of KRW 137.5 billion translated into a 36.4% gross margin, flat year-on-year despite higher platform fees, thanks to the shift from paid advertising to community-driven virality after launch windows close. Operating expenses fell 22.9% sequentially as Q4’s marketing blitz for THE RAGNAROK normalized, lifting operating income back to 18% of sales. Net income margin of 16% still trails the 20% levels seen in pandemic-era peaks, yet the trajectory is improving.

Cash and short-term instruments totaled KRW 577.2 billion (US$391.4 million) at quarter-end, with no debt. That equates to roughly US$56 per ADR, covering 90% of today’s US$62.38 share price. Enterprise value is therefore just US$34 million, or 0.09 times trailing revenue and 0.45 times EBITDA. Operating cash flow has exceeded net income in five of the past six years, and capex runs below US$2 million annually, so free cash conversion stays north of 85%.

Management does not provide explicit quarterly guidance, but its May presentation reiterates an objective of “stable double-digit” revenue growth for 2025 driven by nine scheduled launches and geographic expansion. The Q1 run-rate already annualizes to US$373 million, implying that guidance is achievable with only low-single-digit sequential growth in the remaining quarters.

Risks

Ragnarok concentration remains the biggest swing factor. While multiple genres cushion the blow, any material decline in core loyalty could pressure margins because brand licensing fees are largely fixed. A second vector is platform risk: Gravity earns 85% of revenue from Android and iOS stores, so policy changes around IDFA or third-party billing could raise commission expense.

Regulatory exposure is meaningful. Titles slated for China releases require ISBN approval, and Beijing’s unpredictable licensing cadence can delay monetization. Although Ragnarok Landverse’s blockchain angle targets the crypto-friendly RONIN platform, a global backlash against play-to-earn could stall uptake. Currency is another watch-out: 75% of costs are in Korean won while 60% of revenue originates in US dollars or Thai baht, so a sharp KRW appreciation would compress gross margin. Finally, GungHo’s majority stake means minority holders have limited influence over dividends or buybacks, a concern highlighted in prior activist letters.

Conclusion

At US$62 per share, Gravity trades at 7.6 times trailing earnings, a PEG of 0.61, just 0.45 times EV/EBITDA and 1.18 times sales. NetEase (NTES), which also marries first-party development with publishing, changes hands at 19 times earnings, 13.9 times EV/EBITDA and 5.7 times sales. Roblox (RBLX), a purer UGC platform but a helpful benchmark for mobile engagement, fetches a 17.98 times sales multiple despite negative earnings. Even if Gravity merely rerates halfway toward NetEase on EV/EBITDA, the implied target is US$125 per share, double current levels, before assigning any option value to AI or blockchain initiatives.

The market’s skepticism stems from single-franchise dependence and governance optics, yet the numbers tell a different story. Ragnarok’s flywheel keeps spinning, the pipeline is broader than ever, and a net-cash balance equal to 90% of equity provides a built-in margin of safety. With high-teens ROE, consistent free cash generation and a valuation that prices in permanent decay, Gravity offers a classic mispricing. I remain firmly bullish and expect the stock to outperform global gaming peers as new releases roll through the model and investors rediscover the compounding power of an IP-rich platform bought at cash-box prices.
